plant.description:
Botanical Family: The provided sources do not specify the botanical plant family for peanuts. (Note: Based on general botanical knowledge outside the provided sources, peanuts are technically legumes belonging to the pea/bean family, Fabaceae (or Leguminosae). You may want to independently verify this information).
2. Brief Description and Common Forms
Description: Peanuts are a popular human snack food. While they do not contain a specific inherent toxin like macadamia nuts do, they still pose several distinct health risks to pets. The sources note that peanuts can trigger a potentially deadly allergic reaction1. Furthermore, peanuts that become moldy can harbor Aspergillus fungi, which produce aflatoxins; if ingested, this leads to aflatoxicosis, causing severe liver toxicity and delayed growth34. Finally, because human peanut snacks are often heavily salted, feeding them to pets can lead to dangerous excess sodium intake, dehydration, and salt poisoning2.
Common Forms: In households, pets most frequently encounter peanuts as whole roasted/salted nuts2 or processed into peanut butter5.... Pet owners must be exceptionally cautious with peanut butter, as many "sugar-free" or "reduced-sugar" brands are sweetened with xylitol, an artificial sugar substitute that is exquisitely toxic to pets and can cause fatal hypoglycemia and liver failure5....

toxins.name: While peanuts do not contain a specific, inherent chemical poison in the same way that onions or chocolate do
Aflatoxins (Mold Contamination)
toxins.description:
in cats: Peanuts are highly susceptible to becoming damp and developing Aspergillus mold, which produces aflatoxins14. If an animal ingests moldy peanuts, it causes aflatoxicosis. This disrupts normal cellular function, leading to severe liver damage, ruptured blood vessels (internal bleeding), and jaundice15.
Any notes on concentration or potency: The severity depends on the dose; high doses result in acute, severe liver damage and sudden death, while prolonged low-dose exposure causes reduced growth rates and liver enlargement
toxins.concentration_notes: or potency: The severity depends on the dose; high doses result in acute, severe liver damage and sudden death, while prolonged low-dose exposure causes reduced growth rates and liver enlargement

toxins.name: Peanut Allergens
toxins.description:
in cats: The specific proteins in peanuts can trigger a severe immune system overreaction. The sources note that peanuts can specifically cause a "deadly allergic reaction" in pets3.
Any notes on concentration or potency: Allergic reactions are not strictly dose-dependent but rely on the individual animal's specific immune sensitivity
toxins.concentration_notes: or potency: Allergic reactions are not strictly dose-dependent but rely on the individual animal's specific immune sensitivity

toxins.name: Xylitol (Found in Peanut Butter)
toxins.description:
in cats: Many commercial peanut butters use xylitol as an artificial, sugar-free sweetener26. In pets, xylitol tricks the pancreas into releasing a massive amount of insulin, which causes a rapid, life-threatening drop in blood sugar (hypoglycemia) and potential acute liver failure78. While dogs are exquisitely sensitive to this, and clinical data on cats is less conclusive, veterinary toxicologists strongly advise keeping all xylitol out of a cat's reach as it may still cause liver damage89.
Any notes on concentration or potency: Xylitol is extremely potent; in dogs, doses as low as 100 mg/kg cause severe hypoglycemia, and doses over 500 mg/kg cause liver failure
toxins.concentration_notes: or potency: Xylitol is extremely potent; in dogs, doses as low as 100 mg/kg cause severe hypoglycemia, and doses over 500 mg/kg cause liver failure

toxins.name: High Fats and Added Salt (Processed Peanuts)
toxins.description:
in cats: Peanuts are naturally very high in oils and fats, and human peanut snacks are frequently seasoned with high amounts of salt4.... A cat's digestive system cannot easily process dense plant fats, which leads to severe indigestion, diarrhea, and the potential to trigger pancreatitis (a painful inflammation of the pancreas)1314. Furthermore, the heavy salt content increases the metabolic burden on the cat's kidneys and can lead to sodium ion poisoning, disrupting electrolyte balances and causing tremors and seizures415.
Any notes on concentration or potency: The sources note that large amounts of salt can be fatal to pets1516. Treats containing these ingredients should make up no more than 10% of a pet's daily caloric intake, though peanuts are advised against entirely
toxins.concentration_notes: or potency: The sources note that large amounts of salt can be fatal to pets1516. Treats containing these ingredients should make up no more than 10% of a pet's daily caloric intake, though peanuts are advised against entirely

symptoms.name: While plain, raw peanuts do not contain a specific inherent chemical poison
Aflatoxicosis (From Moldy Peanuts)
symptoms.onset: Acute (shortly after a large dose) or chronic (developing progressively over time with low-dose expo
symptoms.notes: Peanuts are susceptible to growing Aspergillus fungi, which produce toxic aflatoxins1. Ingestion of these moldy peanuts results in severe liver damage and the rupturing of blood vessels

symptoms.name: Hypoglycemia and Liver Failure (From Xylitol in Peanut Butter)
symptoms.onset: 15 to 30 minutes for hypoglycemia (or delayed up to 12 hours depending on the product);
symptoms.notes: Many peanut butters are sweetened with xylitol, an artificial sweetener that tricks the body into releasing a massive amount of insulin37. While clinical data suggests cats are more resistant to xylitol than dogs, it can still cause a rapid drop in blood sugar and potentially fatal liver damage, requiring immediate emergency veterinary care

symptoms.name: Sodium Ion Poisoning (From Salted Peanuts)
symptoms.onset: Within minutes to hours of ingestion
symptoms.notes: Human peanut snacks are frequently heavily seasoned with salt, which unnecessarily increases the metabolic burden on a cat's kidneys4. An overdose of sodium draws fluid out of the body's cells, resulting in severe dehydration, electrolyte derangement, and potentially fatal kidney failure or cerebral damage

symptoms.name: Indigestion and Pancreatitis (From High Fat Content)
symptoms.onset: Within a few hours (for standard indigestion) or progressively over days (for pancreatitis)
symptoms.notes: Peanuts are rich in oils and have a hard physical texture that easily leads to indigestion and diarrhea in cats4. Furthermore, consuming high-fat foods can trigger pancreatitis, a painful and potentially life-threatening inflammation of the pancreas

symptoms.name: Severe Allergic Reaction
symptoms.notes: Peanuts have been specifically noted as a food item capable of triggering a deadly allergic reaction in pets

treatments.name: Because peanuts do not contain a specific inherent chemical poison
Rapid Hypoglycemia Management & Decontamination (If Xylitol-sweetened peanut butter was ingested)
treatments.description:
of the procedure: If the ingestion is caught very early (within a couple of hours) and the cat has not yet developed symptoms, the veterinarian may administer a specific injection to induce vomiting (emesis) to empty the stomach and minimize absorption1. If the cat is already showing symptoms of low blood sugar, they will be admitted for intensive in-patient monitoring and administered IV fluids containing sugar (dextrose) directly into their veins2.
Any situation-specific
treatments.notes: Pet owners must never attempt to induce vomiting in cats at home, as there are no safe methods to do so without professional supervision3. While studies suggest cats are much more resistant to xylitol than dogs, any ingestion of this artificial sweetener requires prompt veterinary care as a precaution against potentially fatal drops in blood sugar and liver damage

treatments.name: Controlled Sodium Correction (If heavily salted peanuts were ingested)
treatments.description:
of the procedure: The veterinarian will measure the cat's serum sodium and electrolyte levels and administer specific IV fluids to help dilute the sodium in the blood. The goal is to slowly return the animal to a normal water and electrolyte balance over a period of 2 to 3 days6....
Any situation-specific
treatments.notes: It is critical that the veterinarian lowers the sodium concentration slowly. If fluids are given too rapidly, it increases the osmotic gradient between the blood and the brain, drawing water into the brain cells and causing severe, potentially fatal cerebral edema (brain swelling)

treatments.name: Emergency Anaphylaxis Response (If an allergic reaction occurs)
treatments.description:
of the procedure: If the cat experiences a severe immune overreaction, the vet will provide immediate life-saving stabilization (such as airway support, oxygen, and emergency medications) to counteract the systemic allergic response.
Any situation-specific
treatments.notes: Peanuts have been specifically identified as a food capable of triggering a "deadly allergic reaction" in pets

treatments.name: Intensive Medical Management for Pancreatitis (If triggered by high fat content)
treatments.description:
of the procedure: If the high fat in the peanuts triggers pancreatitis, the cat will be hospitalized and given IV fluids to correct dehydration and electrolyte abnormalities10. The vet will administer anti-nausea medications (such as maropitant) and prescribe pain relief (such as the opioid buprenorphine)10. To ensure the cat gets vital nutrients, the vet may use appetite stimulants (like mirtazapine) or place a feeding tube11.
Any situation-specific
treatments.notes: Early nutritional support is critical in feline pancreatitis to prevent prolonged anorexia, which can quickly lead to a secondary, life-threatening liver condition known as hepatic lipidosis (fatty liver)
